So what is Fusion? I'm sure most of you will be aware of it, but it's a bit more than you might
think. It is really Autodesk unified design and manufacturing platform.
It allows you to design mechanical components from the simplest of screws to assembling them
to very large assemblies of hundreds or even thousands of bots.
It has a big focus on manufacturing functionality.
Though with it you can define milling and turning tool paths and processes.
It is a relatively manufacturing and even performance section workflows.
Fusion has built in rendering and animation capabilities to visualize

There are ample capabilities to simulate the designs you make to see how they perform

And finally, it has a very sophisticated electronics CAD suite to design, for example,
printed circuit balls. Very importantly, Fusion also comes with an integrated product data management

And all of this is what we call Fusion Industry Cloud.
All the screenshots here on the right are from the Fusion Desktop Cloud, which is one of the

This has been available for about a decade now. It's very sophisticated and stable.
It allows you to essentially automate anything that you can do with the UI.
Either Python or C++, and we are in the process of adding TypeScript as a new language.
Now, more recently, Autodesk, we have developed the Fusion Automation API. It was released
commercially in May of 2025. And it works a bit differently. We essentially run workers,
then virtual machines that run a server version of Fusion in the cloud. And in addition to that,

A couple of other APIs. So I really want to highlight the differences in similarities and how they

on the Fusion Industry Cloud. It is data-centric. So you'll hear on you'll use endpoints or mutations
queries that are focused on getting and setting data. Maybe you can perform. You can also perform

Fusion Industry Cloud. However, the manufacturing data model somewhat limited when it comes to

will always be at a somewhat lower cost. Business models changed, but it will always remain a
more affordable option. Contrast that to the Fusion Automation API. This API is focused on
computing and performing actions on the Fusion Industry Cloud. So the vocabulary is about
jobs or work items. So you will use terms like starting a work item, monitoring its progress,
looking at the results or cancelling long-lasting jobs. With that, naturally, it's somewhat
slower for data read, but it is much more versatile. In particular, you can bring any script that you
would execute usually in the desktop client and all of its power to the automation API. And we

higher cost. Currently, it is charged at three APS tokens per computer. Both of these APIs
